method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
Hashima Island's Japanese name is Hashima (端島), while its widely used nickname is Gunkanjima (軍艦島), commonly translated into English as “Battleship Island”.[2] The nickname combines gunkan, meaning “warship”, with jima, meaning “island” in Japanese.[2] It refers to the island's silhouette, which was compared to a battleship when viewed from a distance.[2] Newspaper accounts helped popularize the name: in 1916, the Osaka Asahi Shimbun reported that Hashima resembled a giant battleship with two chimneys, and in 1921 the Nagasaki Nichinichi Shimbun coined the name Gunkanjima after noting its resemblance to the battleship Tosa.[4]

==History==
Before industrialization, Hashima was a small island associated with local fishing and small-scale coal mining, while nearby Takashima had long supplied exposed coal beds used as household fuel.[1][2] Its later development reflected Japan’s shift from local fuel use to large-scale industrial coal production during the Meiji period and afterward.[2] As the undersea mine expanded, the island was enlarged by landfill and enclosed by seawalls, becoming a compact Mitsubishi company town in which extraction, housing, provisioning and social life were concentrated within hard physical boundaries.[4]

By 1916, Hashima was producing about 150,000 tons of coal annually and had a population of more than 3,000.[2] That year Mitsubishi built a reinforced-concrete apartment block to relieve housing shortages and reduce typhoon damage, a structure described as Japan’s first concrete building of significant size.[2] Over the following decades, the island was built up with dense concrete apartment blocks, schools, medical facilities and entertainment spaces for miners and their families.[1][5] This highly compressed built environment made Hashima both an industrial workplace and a vertically organized residential community.[3][4]

During Japan’s wartime mobilization, Hashima formed part of a wider imperial labor regime in which coercion operated through recruitment systems, policing, confinement, wage discipline and sharply unequal ability to leave, especially for workers marked by colonial status.[4] For this reason, the island’s history has been interpreted not only as a story of modernization and engineering, but also as one involving forced labor.[3][4] In the postwar decades, former residents also remembered Hashima through schools, festivals and dense neighborhood life, memories that later coexisted uneasily with accounts of wartime coercion.[4]

By the 1950s, more than 5,000 people lived on the roughly 16-acre island, making it one of the most densely populated places in the world.[5] In 1959, its overall population density reached about 83,500 people per square kilometre, with the residential district approaching 139,100 people per square kilometre.[3] Hashima’s prosperity depended on coal, and its economic base weakened in the 1960s as petroleum became Japan’s dominant energy source.[5] Mitsubishi closed the mine in 1974, after which residents left the island and its apartments, schools, shops and playgrounds were abandoned.[5]

For decades after abandonment, Hashima’s concrete structures deteriorated under exposure to typhoons, waves, wind, fire and salt air.[1][5] The surviving ruins later became accessible by boat from Nagasaki and were incorporated into heritage and tourism narratives about Japan’s industrial modernization.[1][3] Their interpretation, particularly the place of wartime labor within the island’s history, has remained a source of controversy and diplomatic tension.[1][4]

==Access==
Public access to Hashima Island is limited to authorized guided boat tours, most of which depart from Nagasaki Port.[1][2] Nagasaki Port can be reached from Nagasaki Station by tram to Ohato Station, or from Nagasaki Airport by bus to the same area.[1] Visitors are not permitted to enter the island independently, and access after landing is restricted to designated viewing routes under guide supervision.[2][4]

Landings are frequently affected by sea and weather conditions because the island is exposed offshore.[2] Nagasaki City landing regulations require wave heights below 0.5 metres, wind speeds below 5 metres per second, and visibility greater than 500 metres, although the boat captain may still cancel landing if conditions are judged unsafe.[1] As a result, tour boats are reported to be able to land on the island on only about 100 days per year.[1] Even when boats depart, landing may be cancelled if conditions at the pier deteriorate.[2]

Visitor movement on the island is managed to reduce safety risks and protect the deteriorating structures.[4] On busy days, visitor numbers have exceeded 1,400 per day, with around 260 visitors per hour, but crowding is managed by guides directing groups along the viewing routes.[4] Visitors are not allowed to leave the approved routes, and all access takes place as part of organized tour parties.[4]

==World Heritage Site approval controversy==
Hashima Island was included in Japan’s 23-site nomination for the UNESCO listing ''Sites of Japan’s Meiji Industrial Revolution'', which presented the sites as evidence of Japan’s rapid industrialization from the late nineteenth century.[2][5]

South Korea objected to the nomination on the grounds that seven of the sites were connected to Korean forced labor during the Second World War, and argued that those sites should be excluded from the bid.[1]

Hashima was among the most contentious locations, with Korean critics citing accounts that about 600 Koreans worked in its coal mines and that many died from exposure or overwork.[1]

Japanese proponents of the nomination argued that the World Heritage framework focused on the period from 1850 to 1910, before wartime mobilization from the Korean Peninsula, while noting Hashima’s relevance to the nominated industrial history because Mitsubishi acquired the island’s coal mines in 1890.[3]

UNESCO approved the Meiji industrial sites, including Hashima, as World Heritage sites in 2015, but the decision drew criticism in South Korea over how the sites’ wartime labor history would be represented.[3][5]

Some critics argued that World Heritage status itself was not necessarily inappropriate, but that Hashima should be interpreted as a place for historical self-examination rather than solely as a celebration of industrial achievement.[5]

The dispute continued after approval, especially after the Industrial Heritage Information Centre opened to the public in 2020; South Korean Foreign Minister Kang Kyung-wha told UNESCO that its explanations concerning people from the former Korean Peninsula were insufficient and asked UNESCO to consider removing Hashima’s World Heritage status.[3]

Supporters of the Japanese interpretation, including Industrial Heritage Information Centre managing director Koko Kato, said the centre had gathered extensive primary materials and testimonies from former Hashima residents, while maintaining that Japanese and South Korean views of the issue remained sharply opposed.[3]

==Current status==
The island remains uninhabited, with empty high-rise buildings, seawalls and other mine-era structures exposed to wind and salt spray.[5] Contemporary travel writing and tourism media commonly present Hashima as an abandoned industrial landscape and a destination associated with heritage tourism, photography and urban exploration.[2][4] Its ruins have also become a subject of academic and creative work, including the AHRC-linked project *The Future of Ruins: Reclaiming Abandonment and Toxicity on Hashima Island* and the related 2013 performance *Return to Battleship Island*.[3]

Hashima is widely circulated through popular culture and digital media, including fictionalized associations with *Skyfall* and survival-horror games.[4] A survey of immersive and virtual-reality projects found that such projects often emphasize either the former community life of the island or the visual spectacle of decay, while tending to avoid contested aspects of the site’s history.[4] Educational and heritage-oriented digital resources have used Hashima to examine contested heritage, UNESCO inscription, national memory politics and commercial tourism.[4]

The island’s interpretation continues to be the subject of diplomatic disagreement.[1] At the 47th session of the UNESCO World Heritage Committee in Paris, South Korea sought renewed examination of Japan’s follow-up measures regarding Hashima, while Japan argued that it had implemented earlier resolutions.[1] The committee backed the position that disputes over Hashima should be addressed through bilateral dialogue rather than UNESCO procedures.[1] Japan’s revised proposal was adopted by anonymous ballot, with seven votes in favor, three against and several abstentions among the 21 committee members.[1] Following that outcome, further formal discussion of the issue during the session, or in future committee meetings, was reported as unlikely.[1]

==In popular culture==
Hashima Island’s abandoned landscape has become a recurring image in film, television, games and digital media, often detached from the island’s archival and historical record.[2] It is widely associated with the James Bond film *Skyfall* (2012), in which its ruins were used as the visual model for Raoul Silva’s lair.[2][4] The island also appeared in National Geographic’s documentary series *One Strange Rock*.[4]

Scholars have argued that such representations shape public memory of Hashima by emphasizing spectacle, decay and adventure over the island’s contested wartime history.[1] One study of the period after *Skyfall* argued that the circulation of Hashima imagery in popular culture can complicate efforts to secure recognition of past abuses connected to the site.[1] The same study examined Google Street View imagery of Hashima as part of a broader shift toward digital heritage, virtual navigation and mediated spatial experience.[1]

Hashima has also been represented in horror and immersive media, including the survival-horror game *The Closed Ward* (2018) and related works that use the island’s ruins as an atmospheric setting.[2] A survey of eleven publicly accessible virtual and immersive Hashima projects found that they ranged from early stereoscopic prototypes and museum installations to university scanning projects, VRChat worlds and commercial walking simulators.[2] That survey argued that these projects tended to focus either on nostalgic depictions of community life or on picturesque ruin, rather than fully engaging with the island’s more difficult historical controversies.[2]
